excluded_defs.reason: verified it calls next(cancellationToken) but no changes needed — just confirming compatibility

exploration_log.aha_moment: CancellationToken.None == default(CancellationToken), so the old `== default` check is inherently ambiguous. Nullable types provide the exact distinction needed.
exploration_log.hindsight: Could have immediately jumped to the delegate definition to understand the full scope; the fix in IPipelineBehavior.cs is the root change that enables the fix in RequestHandlerWrapper.cs.

alternatives: Use a private wrapper struct to encode 'not set'
Use a bool flag alongside CancellationToken
key_decisions.decision: Change delegate signature to CancellationToken? (nullable) instead of using a sentinel value
key_decisions.reasoning: CancellationToken? cleanly distinguishes null (not provided) from CancellationToken.None (explicit). It's idiomatic C#, backward-compatible since CancellationToken implicitly converts to CancellationToken?, and is the simplest approach.

search_sequence.reasoning: Task description pointed directly to this file as the location of the bug

search_sequence.reasoning: Need to understand the delegate signature to change it to nullable
search_sequence.result: Found `RequestHandlerDelegate<TResponse>` declared as `delegate Task<TResponse> RequestHandlerDelegate<TResponse>(CancellationToken t = default)`

search_sequence.reasoning: Needed to confirm changing to nullable wouldn't break callers; CancellationToken implicitly converts to CancellationToken?
search_sequence.result: All four pipeline behaviors call `next(cancellationToken)` — passing explicit non-null tokens

queries.query_text: fix CancellationToken.None not distinguished from default in RequestHandlerDelegate delegate in RequestHandlerWrapper pipeline

solve_notes: Changed the `RequestHandlerDelegate<TResponse>` delegate signature from `CancellationToken t = default` to `CancellationToken? t = null`. Updated all `Handler` local functions and aggregate lambdas to use `t ?? cancellationToken` instead of `t == default ? cancellationToken : t`. This correctly distinguishes 'not provided' (null) from 'explicitly passed CancellationToken.None' (a non-null value equal to default).
task_text:
Fix Mediator.Send(object) not propagating CancellationToken to the pipeline

In `src/MediatR/Mediator.cs`, the `Send(object request, CancellationToken)`
method resolves the wrapper via `_requestHandlers.GetOrAdd` and calls
`handler.Handle(request, _serviceProvider, cancellationToken)`. However,
in `Wrappers/RequestHandlerWrapper.cs`, the `RequestHandlerWrapperImpl<TRequest, TResponse>.Handle(object, ...)`
overload calls `Handle((IRequest<TResponse>)request, serviceProvider, cancellationToken)`
which constructs a `Handler` delegate. The inner `Handler` delegate uses
`t == default ? cancellationToken : t` comparison, but when `t` is
`CancellationToken.None` (the default parameter value from the delegate
signature), it incorrectly falls back to the outer token even when the
caller explicitly passed `CancellationToken.None`. Fix the delegate
parameter handling to distinguish between "not provided" and "explicitly
None".

thrash_preventing_defs.name: IPipelineBehavior
thrash_preventing_defs.reason: read: understanding IPipelineBehavior.Handle signature confirms pipeline behaviors pass explicit CancellationToken to next, ensuring nullable change is backward-compatible

tier_difference_reasoning: The minimum set covers exactly what was edited. The thrash-preventing set adds IPipelineBehavior because an AI agent would want to verify that changing the delegate parameter type doesn't break the contract that pipeline behaviors depend on — specifically, that calling `next(cancellationToken)` still compiles when the parameter is CancellationToken?. Without seeing this upfront, the agent might search all pipeline behavior files one-by-one checking compatibility.
